---
title: "Forecast Evaluation"
subtitle: "BSAD 8310: Business Forecasting --- Lecture 6"
author: "Department of Economics"
institute: "University of Nebraska at Omaha"
date: "Spring 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, emory-clean.scss]
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    incremental: false
    toc: false
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.05
    logo: ""
    footer: "BSAD 8310 | University of Nebraska at Omaha | Spring 2026"
    embed-resources: true
bibliography: ../Bibliography_base.bib
csl: https://www.zotero.org/styles/chicago-author-date
---

## Lecture 6: Outline

1. The Evaluation Problem
2. Error Metrics
3. Walk-Forward Validation
4. The Diebold-Mariano Test
5. Forecast Combination
6. Key Takeaways and Roadmap

---

# The Evaluation Problem

Five model families, one retail series --- how do we know which one to use?

---

## The Single-Split Trap

Every lab in Lectures 1--5 held out a single final window of $H$ periods.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Three failure modes:**

1. **Lucky split:** one origin may favor one model by chance
2. **Test-set leakage:** tuning on the test set inflates performance
3. **Horizon conflation:** $h=1$ and $h=12$ accuracy are very different --- averaging hides the pattern

::: {.warningbox}
::: {.smaller}
A single split is a photograph. Walk-forward evaluation is a movie.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.column width="46%"}
**What rigorous evaluation requires:**

- Multiple forecast origins (not one $T$)
- Horizon-specific accuracy ($h=1,\ldots,H$)
- A loss function matched to business costs
- Statistical testing to separate signal from noise

[*This lecture delivers all four. (See L01 footnote: "walk-forward evaluation: Lecture 6.")*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::::

---

## Our Model Zoo

Before evaluating, a map of what we now have:

::: {.smaller}

| **Family** | **Lecture** | **Strength** | **Weakness** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Benchmarks | L1 | Simple, fast | No dynamics |
| Regression / AR | L2 | Interpretable | Assumes linearity |
| ETS / Exp. smooth. | L3 | Trend & seasonality | Univariate only |
| ARIMA / SARIMA | L4 | Box-Jenkins; flexible | Complex identification |
| VAR / ARIMAX / ECM | L5 | Multivariate dynamics | Parameter-heavy |

:::

::: {.keybox}
Evaluation is the final step in the modeling cycle: build $\to$ fit $\to$ **evaluate out-of-sample** $\to$ decide. No model is universally best across all series, horizons, and loss functions.
:::

---

# Error Metrics

RMSE, MAE, MAPE, and MASE each answer a different business question.

---

## Forecast Accuracy Metrics

Let $e_{T+h} = y_{T+h} - \hat{y}_{T+h|T}$.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Four Metrics
:::
::: {.smaller}
$$\text{RMSE}_h = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{n}\sum e_{T+h}^2} \quad \textit{scale-dep.}$$

$$\text{MAE}_h = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum|e_{T+h}| \quad \textit{scale-dep.}$$

$$\text{MAPE}_h = \tfrac{100}{n}\sum\left|\frac{e_{T+h}}{y_{T+h}}\right| \quad \textit{scale-free}$$

$$\text{MASE}_h = \text{MAE}_h \,/\, \bar{q} \quad \textit{scale-free}$$

[$\bar{q}$ = in-sample MAE of seasonal naïve.]{.neutral .smaller}
:::
:::
:::

::: {.column width="46%"}
::: {.keybox}
::: {.smaller}
**Decision guide:**

RMSE: large errors costly

MAE: equal cost per unit

MASE $<1$: beats naïve

MAPE: only when $y_t \gg 0$

Never report RMSE alone --- show MAE alongside.
:::
:::
:::

::::

[[@Hyndman2006] *Socratic: two series, RMSE(A) $<$ RMSE(B) but MASE(A) $>$ MASE(B). Which model is "better"?*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## RMSE vs. MAE: Align with Business Costs

RMSE penalizes large errors quadratically; MAE treats all errors equally.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**When RMSE $\gg$ MAE:**

- Error distribution has heavy tails
- Occasional very large misses
- If those misses are costly $\Rightarrow$ RMSE is the right metric

```{=html}
<svg width="100%" viewBox="0 0 260 140" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-height:140px; margin-top:0.5em;">
  <!-- Axes -->
  <line x1="30" y1="120" x2="250" y2="120" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="30" y1="120" x2="30"  y2="10"  stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- X label -->
  <text x="240" y="134" font-size="9" fill="#525252" font-style="italic">error</text>
  <!-- Y label -->
  <text x="5" y="70" font-size="9" fill="#525252" font-style="italic" transform="rotate(-90,12,70)">Freq.</text>
  <!-- Bars: x bins -4 to 4, heights: 1,2,5,9,14,8,5,2,3 -->
  <!-- bar width ~20px, spacing ~22px, start x=35 -->
  <!-- heights scaled: max=14 -> 90px bar = top at y=30 -->
  <!-- scale: h px = val * 6.4 -->
  <rect x="33"  y="113" width="16" height="7"  fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <rect x="55"  y="107" width="16" height="13" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <rect x="77"  y="88"  width="16" height="32" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <rect x="99"  y="62"  width="16" height="58" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <rect x="121" y="30"  width="16" height="90" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <rect x="143" y="69"  width="16" height="51" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <rect x="165" y="88"  width="16" height="32" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <rect x="187" y="107" width="16" height="13" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <rect x="209" y="100" width="16" height="19" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <!-- X tick labels -->
  <text x="41"  y="132" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">-4</text>
  <text x="85"  y="132" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">-2</text>
  <text x="129" y="132" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">0</text>
  <text x="173" y="132" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">2</text>
  <text x="217" y="132" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">4</text>
</svg>
```
:::

::: {.column width="46%"}
::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
Retail inventory cost
:::
::: {.smaller}
Stockout cost $= 5\times$ overstock cost. The loss function is *asymmetric* and favors penalizing large underestimates.

$\Rightarrow$ RMSE aligns better than MAE.

$\Rightarrow$ Ideally: use asymmetric loss $L(e) = c_1 e^{+} + c_2 e^{-}$, where $e^{+}=\max(e,0)$, $e^{-}=\max(-e,0)$.
:::
:::

[*Never report only RMSE --- always show MAE to reveal whether accuracy is driven by a few outlier periods.*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::::

---

## MAPE Pitfalls and When to Use MASE

**MAPE failure cases:**

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
- $y_{T+h} \approx 0$ $\Rightarrow$ MAPE $\to \infty$ (e.g., new product launch, zero-demand months)
- **Asymmetric:** when $y>0$, under-forecasts contribute at most 100% to MAPE (bounded: $\hat{y}=0 \Rightarrow |e/y|=1$); over-forecasts are unbounded above ($\hat{y}\to\infty \Rightarrow$ MAPE $\to\infty$)
- Percentage errors favor low-variance series
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
::: {.keybox}
::: {.smaller}
**MASE** [@Hyndman2006]:

$\text{MASE} < 1$ $\Rightarrow$ beats seasonal naïve

$\text{MASE} > 1$ $\Rightarrow$ worse than naïve

Works for intermittent demand, zero values, and cross-series comparison.
:::
:::
:::

::::

**Practical recommendation:** report RMSE + MAE for absolute accuracy; MASE for benchmarking; MAPE only when $y_t \gg 0$.

[*Socratic: a new product launches in month 1 with $y_1=0$. Which metric can you* not *compute, and what do you use instead?*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## Accuracy by Horizon

**Key insight:** the winner at $h=1$ is not always the winner at $h=12$.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}

```{=html}
<svg width="100%" viewBox="0 0 300 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-height:200px;">
  <!-- Axes -->
  <line x1="40" y1="170" x2="280" y2="170" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="40" y1="170" x2="40"  y2="20"  stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- Y axis label -->
  <text x="8" y="100" font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90,8,100)">RMSE (normalized)</text>
  <!-- X axis label -->
  <text x="285" y="174" font-size="9" fill="#525252" font-style="italic">h</text>
  <!-- Y ticks: 0.6 -> y=170, 2.0 -> y=20; scale=(170-20)/(2.0-0.6)=107 per unit -->
  <!-- 0.6=170, 1.0=127, 1.4=84, 1.8=41 -->
  <line x1="38" y1="127" x2="42" y2="127" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="36" y="130" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="end">1.0</text>
  <line x1="38" y1="84"  x2="42" y2="84"  stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="36" y="87"  font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="end">1.4</text>
  <!-- X ticks: h=1,3,6,9,12; x = 40 + (h-1)*21.8 -->
  <!-- h=1->40, h=2->61.8, h=3->83.6, h=6->149, h=9->214.4, h=12->279.8 -->
  <text x="40"  y="182" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">1</text>
  <text x="84"  y="182" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">3</text>
  <text x="149" y="182" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">6</text>
  <text x="214" y="182" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">9</text>
  <text x="280" y="182" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">12</text>

  <!-- Naïve: y = 0.9 + 0.09*h; at h=1: 0.99->163, h=12: 1.98->43 -->
  <!-- polyline: h=1..12 -->
  <polyline
    points="40,163 62,161 84,158 106,155 127,152 149,149 171,146 193,143 214,140 236,137 258,134 280,131"
    fill="none" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6,4"/>
  <!-- Naïve legend -->
  <line x1="45" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6,4"/>
  <text x="73" y="38" font-size="8" fill="#525252">Naïve</text>

  <!-- SARIMA: y = 0.72 + 0.04*h + 0.002*h^2 -->
  <!-- h=1:0.762->143, h=2:0.808->137, h=3:0.858->129, h=4:0.912->121, h=5:0.97->113 -->
  <!-- h=6:1.032->104, h=7:1.098->94, h=8:1.168->85, h=9:1.242->75, h=10:1.32->65 -->
  <!-- h=11:1.402->54, h=12:1.488->43 -->
  <polyline
    points="40,143 62,137 84,130 106,122 127,114 149,104 171,94 193,85 214,75 236,65 258,54 280,43"
    fill="none" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="2.2"/>
  <!-- SARIMA legend -->
  <line x1="45" y1="48" x2="70" y2="48" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="2.2"/>
  <text x="73" y="51" font-size="8" fill="#005CA9">SARIMA</text>

  <!-- ETS: y = 0.74 + 0.035*h + 0.003*h^2 -->
  <!-- h=1:0.778->141, h=2:0.826->135, h=3:0.877->128, h=4:0.932->120, h=5:0.99->112 -->
  <!-- h=6:1.053->103, h=7:1.12->93, h=8:1.192->83, h=9:1.267->73, h=10:1.347->62 -->
  <!-- h=11:1.43->51, h=12:1.516->39 -->
  <polyline
    points="40,141 62,135 84,128 106,120 127,112 149,103 171,93 193,83 214,73 236,62 258,51 280,39"
    fill="none" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="2.2"/>
  <!-- ETS legend -->
  <line x1="45" y1="61" x2="70" y2="61" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="2.2"/>
  <text x="73" y="64" font-size="8" fill="#15803d">ETS</text>
</svg>
```

:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
***Always report accuracy by horizon, not just as a single average.***

**Why profiles diverge:**

- At $h=1$: model captures short-run dynamics
- At $h=12$: model must extrapolate seasonal patterns
- Naïve accumulates error with horizon; ARIMA/ETS maintain seasonal anchor

[*Lab 6 plots this profile on RSXFS with 36 walk-forward origins.*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::::

---

# Walk-Forward Validation

A single test set yields one error estimate. Walk-forward validation yields a distribution of errors across forecast origins.

---

## The Walk-Forward Idea

At each **forecast origin** $t$: fit model on $\{y_1,\ldots,y_t\}$, forecast $\{y_{t+1},\ldots,y_{t+H}\}$.

```{=html}
<svg width="100%" viewBox="0 0 580 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-height:200px;">
  <!-- === EXPANDING WINDOW LABEL === -->
  <text x="10" y="42" font-size="11" fill="#1a1a1a" font-weight="bold">Expanding:</text>

  <!-- Row 1: train [0,160], forecast [160,200] -->
  <rect x="90"  y="28" width="160" height="22" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <rect x="250" y="28" width="40"  height="22" fill="#FBD3D8" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <text x="170" y="43" font-size="9" fill="#005CA9" text-anchor="middle">Train</text>
  <text x="270" y="43" font-size="9" fill="#E41C38" text-anchor="middle">Fcst</text>

  <!-- Row 2: train [0,230], forecast [230,270] -->
  <rect x="90"  y="60" width="230" height="22" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <rect x="320" y="60" width="40"  height="22" fill="#FBD3D8" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="0.8"/>

  <!-- Row 3: train [0,300], forecast [300,340] -->
  <rect x="90"  y="92" width="300" height="22" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <rect x="390" y="92" width="40"  height="22" fill="#FBD3D8" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="0.8"/>

  <!-- === ROLLING WINDOW LABEL === -->
  <text x="10" y="148" font-size="11" fill="#1a1a1a" font-weight="bold">Rolling:</text>

  <!-- Row 4: train [0,160], forecast [160,200] -->
  <rect x="90"  y="134" width="160" height="22" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <rect x="250" y="134" width="40"  height="22" fill="#FBD3D8" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <text x="170" y="149" font-size="9" fill="#005CA9" text-anchor="middle">Train</text>
  <text x="270" y="149" font-size="9" fill="#E41C38" text-anchor="middle">Fcst</text>

  <!-- Row 5: train [70,230], forecast [230,270] -->
  <rect x="160" y="162" width="160" height="22" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <rect x="320" y="162" width="40"  height="22" fill="#FBD3D8" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="0.8"/>

  <!-- Row 6: train [140,300], forecast [300,340] -->
  <rect x="230" y="190" width="160" height="22" fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <rect x="390" y="190" width="40"  height="22" fill="#FBD3D8" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="0.8"/>

  <!-- Time axis -->
  <line x1="90" y1="220" x2="450" y2="220" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <polygon points="450,216 460,220 450,224" fill="#525252"/>
  <text x="464" y="224" font-size="9" fill="#525252" font-style="italic">Time t</text>
  <text x="90"  y="232" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">1</text>
  <text x="445" y="232" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">T</text>
</svg>
```

[***Expanding:*** train grows at each origin. ***Rolling:*** fixed-size window shifts forward. Both produce a time series of forecast errors per horizon $h$.]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## Rolling vs. Expanding: When to Use Which

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Use expanding window when:**

- Data-generating process is *stable* over time
- More training data $\to$ better parameters
- Long series with no structural breaks

**Use rolling window when:**

- Structural breaks (e.g., COVID-19 shock)
- Time-varying parameters / regime change
- Fixed window forces model to "forget" old patterns
:::

::: {.column width="46%"}
::: {.warningbox}
::: {.smaller}
**Minimum training size:** choose $T_0$ large enough for reliable estimation. For SARIMA$(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_m$: at least $3m + p + P$ observations.

For monthly data: $T_0 \geq 36$ months.
:::
:::

**Python:** `TimeSeriesSplit` in `sklearn.model_selection` implements expanding-window CV; rolling requires a manual loop.
:::

::::

---

## Walk-Forward Validation: Step-by-Step

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
1. Choose: first origin $T_0$, window type, horizon $H$
2. **For** each origin $t = T_0, T_0+1, \ldots, T-H$:
   a. Fit model on $\{y_1,\ldots,y_t\}$
   b. Generate forecasts $\hat{y}_{t+h|t}$, $h=1,\ldots,H$
   c. Store errors $e_{t,h} = y_{t+h} - \hat{y}_{t+h|t}$
3. Average over origins per horizon:
$$\text{RMSE}_h = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{n_h}\sum_t e_{t,h}^2}$$
4. Plot horizon profile; run DM test for significance
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
*Walk-forward errors are the closest thing to a **live forecast track record**: each $e_{t,h}$ represents the error you would have made using this model in real time.*

[*$e_{t,h}$ generalizes $e_{T+h}$ from the Error Metrics section to multiple origins $t$; the metric formulas are the same, averaged over $t$. Number of usable origins at horizon $h$: $n_h = T - T_0 - h + 1$; at the maximum horizon $h=H$: $n_H = T - T_0 - H + 1$; with monthly data, 24--36 origins is typical.*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::::

---

## Walk-Forward Results: Model Zoo on RSXFS

**Illustrative results** (36 expanding-window origins, $H=12$):

::: {.smaller}

| **Model** | **RMSE$_{h=1}$** | **RMSE$_{h=3}$** | **RMSE$_{h=12}$** | **MASE** |
|:---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Naïve (seasonal) | 2,810 | 3,145 | 4,210 | 1.00 |
| SARIMA(1,1,1)(0,1,1) | 1,720 | 2,040 | 3,480 | 0.76 |
| ETS (auto-AIC) | 1,690 | 2,020 | 3,510 | 0.75 |
| ARIMAX (+ sentiment) | 1,640 | 1,980 | 3,530 | 0.73 |
| VAR (BIC order) | 1,660 | 2,100 | 3,620 | 0.77 |
| **Equal-weight combo** | **1,600** | **1,920** | **3,390** | **0.71** |

:::

[*Combination beats all individual models across every horizon. Is the ETS vs. SARIMA gap statistically real? $\to$ Section 4.*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

# The Diebold-Mariano Test

Visual inspection says Model A looks better. The DM test asks: is the gap statistically real?

---

## DM Test: Loss Differentials

To compare two models, examine the **loss differential** at each origin.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Loss Differential
:::
::: {.smaller}
For each origin $t$, let:
$$d_t = g(e_{1t}) - g(e_{2t})$$
where $g(\cdot)$ is any loss function.

For MSE loss: $d_t = e_{1t}^2 - e_{2t}^2$.

Average loss differential:
$$\bar{d} = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^n d_t$$
:::
:::
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
**Numeric example** (MSE loss, $h=1$):

::: {.smaller}
Origin 1: SARIMA error $= 3.2$, ETS error $= 2.8$

$d_1 = 3.2^2 - 2.8^2 = 10.24 - 7.84 = +2.40$

If $\bar{d} > 0$: Model 1 has higher average loss $\Rightarrow$ Model 2 is better.

But is $\bar{d}$ statistically distinguishable from 0?

$\to$ **See next slide.**
:::
:::

::::

---

## DM Test: The Statistic

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Diebold-Mariano Test [@Diebold1995]
:::
::: {.smaller}
$$DM = \frac{\bar{d}}{\widehat{\operatorname{se}}(\bar{d})} \;\xrightarrow{d}\; \mathcal{N}(0,1)$$

$\bar{d}$: average loss differential (see prev. slide); $\widehat{\operatorname{se}}(\bar{d})$: HAC standard error.

$H_0$: equal predictive accuracy $\bigl(\mathbb{E}[d_t] = 0\bigr)$.
:::
:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="54%"}
**Why HAC?** For an $h$-step forecast, $d_t$ is serially correlated up to lag $h-1$ (overlapping forecast errors). Ignoring this understates the standard error.
:::

::: {.column width="42%"}
::: {.warningbox}
::: {.smaller}
Use Newey-West bandwidth $= h-1$ for the HAC variance; with $h=1$ (one-step forecasts), $d_t$ is white noise under $H_0$.
:::
:::
:::

::::

---

## Interpreting the DM Test

**SARIMA vs. ETS on RSXFS** ($h=1$, 36 origins, MSE loss):

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="56%"}

```{=html}
<svg width="100%" viewBox="0 0 320 180" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-height:180px;">
  <!-- Axes -->
  <line x1="40" y1="140" x2="305" y2="140" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="40" y1="140" x2="40"  y2="10"  stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- Y axis label -->
  <text x="8" y="80" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90,8,80)">d_t</text>
  <!-- X axis label -->
  <text x="310" y="144" font-size="8" fill="#525252" font-style="italic">origin</text>
  <!-- Y ticks: -3 -> y=?, 0 -> y=?, 3 -> y=? -->
  <!-- y range: -3.5 to 4.5, scale=(140-10)/(4.5-(-3.5))=16.25; y=140-(val-(-3.5))*16.25 -->
  <!-- 0 -> 140-3.5*16.25=83.1; 3 -> 140-6.5*16.25=34.4; -3 -> 140-0.5*16.25=131.9 -->
  <line x1="38" y1="83"  x2="42" y2="83"  stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="36" y="86"  font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="end">0</text>
  <line x1="38" y1="34"  x2="42" y2="34"  stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="36" y="37"  font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="end">3</text>
  <line x1="38" y1="132" x2="42" y2="132" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="36" y="135" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="end">-3</text>
  <!-- X ticks: 6,12,18,24,30,36; spacing=(305-40)/36=7.36 per origin; x=40+origin*7.36 -->
  <text x="84"  y="152" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">6</text>
  <text x="128" y="152" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">12</text>
  <text x="172" y="152" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">18</text>
  <text x="216" y="152" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">24</text>
  <text x="261" y="152" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">30</text>
  <text x="305" y="152" font-size="8" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">36</text>

  <!-- Mean line: d_bar=0.25, y=140-(0.25+3.5)*16.25=140-60.9=79.1 -->
  <line x1="40" y1="79" x2="305" y2="79" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5,3"/>
  <text x="270" y="76" font-size="8" fill="#005CA9">d̄=0.25</text>

  <!-- Bars: sin(deg(x*0.9+0.5))*1.8 + 0.25 for x=1..36 -->
  <!-- bar width ~6px, spaced at 7.36 intervals; center x=40+i*7.36 -->
  <!-- val(i)=sin((i*0.9+0.5)*pi/180... wait, the TeX uses deg() which converts to degrees -->
  <!-- sin(deg(x*0.9+0.5)) in pgfplots means sin in degrees of the argument -->
  <!-- Actually pgfplots: {sin(deg(x*0.9+0.5))*1.8+0.25} -->
  <!-- deg(x*0.9+0.5) converts radians to degrees... but x is 1..36 here -->
  <!-- Actually in pgfplots, sin() takes degrees, deg() converts radians to degrees -->
  <!-- So: val = sin(deg(x*0.9+0.5)) where x in [1,36] -->
  <!-- x*0.9+0.5 is in radians, deg() converts: angle_deg = (x*0.9+0.5)*180/pi -->
  <!-- For approximate SVG bars, use computed values -->
  <!-- i=1: sin(deg(1.4))*1.8+0.25 = sin(80.2)*1.8+0.25 = 0.986*1.8+0.25 = 2.02 -->
  <!-- Let me pre-compute a representative sinusoidal pattern for 36 bars -->
  <!-- Using the formula approximately for visual fidelity -->
  <!-- Values (approximate): -->
  <g fill="#B8D4EF" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="0.5">
    <!-- Each bar: x=40+i*7.36-3, width=5; height from 0 line -->
    <!-- y_zero=83; bar top = 83 - val*16.25; if negative val, bar goes down -->
    <!-- i=1: val~2.02, top=83-32.8=50.2, h=32.8 -->
    <rect x="44"  y="50"  width="5" height="33" />
    <!-- i=2: val~1.55 -->
    <rect x="51"  y="58"  width="5" height="25" />
    <!-- i=3: val~0.78 -->
    <rect x="59"  y="70"  width="5" height="13" />
    <!-- i=4: val~-0.10 (negative bar) -->
    <rect x="66"  y="83"  width="5" height="2"  />
    <!-- i=5: val~-0.92 -->
    <rect x="73"  y="83"  width="5" height="15" />
    <!-- i=6: val~-1.52 -->
    <rect x="81"  y="83"  width="5" height="25" />
    <!-- i=7: val~-1.76 -->
    <rect x="88"  y="83"  width="5" height="29" />
    <!-- i=8: val~-1.60 -->
    <rect x="96"  y="83"  width="5" height="26" />
    <!-- i=9: val~-1.07 -->
    <rect x="103" y="83"  width="5" height="17" />
    <!-- i=10: val~-0.28 -->
    <rect x="110" y="83"  width="5" height="5"  />
    <!-- i=11: val~0.58 -->
    <rect x="118" y="74"  width="5" height="9"  />
    <!-- i=12: val~1.33 -->
    <rect x="125" y="61"  width="5" height="22" />
    <!-- i=13: val~1.83 -->
    <rect x="133" y="53"  width="5" height="30" />
    <!-- i=14: val~1.94 -->
    <rect x="140" y="51"  width="5" height="32" />
    <!-- i=15: val~1.63 -->
    <rect x="148" y="56"  width="5" height="26" />
    <!-- i=16: val~1.01 -->
    <rect x="155" y="67"  width="5" height="16" />
    <!-- i=17: val~0.17 -->
    <rect x="162" y="80"  width="5" height="3"  />
    <!-- i=18: val~-0.70 -->
    <rect x="170" y="83"  width="5" height="11" />
    <!-- i=19: val~-1.42 -->
    <rect x="177" y="83"  width="5" height="23" />
    <!-- i=20: val~-1.83 -->
    <rect x="185" y="83"  width="5" height="30" />
    <!-- i=21: val~-1.87 -->
    <rect x="192" y="83"  width="5" height="30" />
    <!-- i=22: val~-1.53 -->
    <rect x="199" y="83"  width="5" height="25" />
    <!-- i=23: val~-0.88 -->
    <rect x="207" y="83"  width="5" height="14" />
    <!-- i=24: val~-0.03 -->
    <rect x="214" y="82"  width="5" height="1"  />
    <!-- i=25: val~0.85 -->
    <rect x="222" y="69"  width="5" height="14" />
    <!-- i=26: val~1.57 -->
    <rect x="229" y="58"  width="5" height="26" />
    <!-- i=27: val~1.97 -->
    <rect x="236" y="51"  width="5" height="32" />
    <!-- i=28: val~1.96 -->
    <rect x="244" y="51"  width="5" height="32" />
    <!-- i=29: val~1.57 -->
    <rect x="251" y="57"  width="5" height="26" />
    <!-- i=30: val~0.84 -->
    <rect x="259" y="69"  width="5" height="14" />
    <!-- i=31: val~-0.02 -->
    <rect x="266" y="82"  width="5" height="1"  />
    <!-- i=32: val~-0.90 -->
    <rect x="273" y="83"  width="5" height="15" />
    <!-- i=33: val~-1.60 -->
    <rect x="281" y="83"  width="5" height="26" />
    <!-- i=34: val~-1.94 -->
    <rect x="288" y="83"  width="5" height="31" />
    <!-- i=35: val~-1.86 -->
    <rect x="296" y="83"  width="5" height="30" />
    <!-- i=36: val~-1.40 (end) -->
    <rect x="303" y="83"  width="5" height="23" />
  </g>
</svg>
```

:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
**Decision rule:**

- $DM > 1.645$ (one-sided): Model 2 significantly better
- $|DM| > 1.96$ (two-sided): models differ significantly
- Fail to reject $H_0$: insufficient evidence that models differ --- do not interpret as evidence of equality

[$\bar{d} > 0$: SARIMA has slightly higher MSE. If $DM > 1.645$, use ETS.]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::::

---

## DM Test: Caveats and Extensions

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="54%"}
**When the DM test applies:**

- Non-nested models (e.g., SARIMA vs. ETS)
- Stationary loss differentials $d_t$
- Sufficient number of origins ($n \geq 20$)

**When it does not apply:**

- **Nested models** (e.g., AR(1) vs. AR(2)): under $H_0$ the DM statistic is non-standard. Use the Clark-West correction [@Clark2007]
- Very small samples ($n < 15$): use finite-sample correction [@Harvey1997]
:::

::: {.column width="42%"}
::: {.keybox}
::: {.smaller}
DM tests **equal expected loss**, not model fit.

Always use *out-of-sample* walk-forward errors --- never in-sample residuals.

Always specify the loss function $g(\cdot)$: MSE, MAE, or asymmetric loss.
:::
:::
:::

::::

[*Socratic: if $\bar{d} > 0$ but $DM = 0.8$, what conclusion do you draw? What would increase power?*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

# Forecast Combination

No single model wins every period. Combining forecasts diversifies model risk.

---

## Why Combine Forecasts?

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="54%"}
**The portfolio analogy:** diversification reduces portfolio variance when assets are imperfectly correlated.
$$\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}e_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}e_2\right) = \tfrac{1}{4}\bigl(\sigma_1^2 + 2\rho\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2\bigr)$$
If $\rho < 1$: the combined error variance is smaller than the average of individual variances.

**Empirical finding:** @BatesGranger1969 showed that weighted combinations *generally* outperform the best individual model in empirical comparisons.
:::

::: {.column width="42%"}
*If two models have equal accuracy but errors are **not perfectly correlated**, the simple average outperforms either model alone.*

[*SARIMA--ETS error correlation $\approx 0.72$ on RSXFS $\Rightarrow$ significant gains from combining.*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::::

---

## Combination Strategies

::: {.smaller}

| **Strategy** | **Formula** | **Key property** | **Practical note** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Equal weights | $\hat{y}^C = \frac{1}{K}\sum_k \hat{y}^{(k)}$ | No estimation needed | Surprisingly robust |
| RMSE weights | $w_k \propto \text{RMSE}_k^{-1}$ | Rewards accuracy | Simple; no overfitting |
| OLS weights | $\hat{w}$ from regressing $y$ on $\hat{y}^{(k)}$ | Optimal in theory | Prone to overfitting |

:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="54%"}
**The combination puzzle** [@Timmermann2006; @StockWatson2004]: estimated OLS weights *often underperform* equal weights out-of-sample. Three reasons:

1. Estimation error in $\hat{w}$
2. Model instability / structural breaks
3. High collinearity among $\hat{y}^{(k)}$
:::

::: {.column width="42%"}
::: {.warningbox}
::: {.smaller}
OLS weights can be negative or $>1$. Constrain to $w_k \geq 0$, $\sum w_k = 1$ in practice. Alternatively: shrink toward equal weights via ridge.
:::
:::
:::

::::

---

## Combination in Practice

**Extending our walk-forward results (RSXFS, 36 origins):**

::: {.smaller}

| **Method** | **RMSE ($h=1$)** | **RMSE ($h=12$)** | **MASE (avg)** |
|:---|---:|---:|---:|
| Best individual (ARIMAX) | 1,640 | 3,530 | 0.73 |
| RMSE-weighted combo | 1,620 | 3,410 | 0.72 |
| **Equal-weight combo** | **1,600** | **3,390** | **0.71** |
| OLS combo (unconstrained) | 1,680 | 3,580 | 0.76 |

:::

::: {.keybox}
**Decision rule:** when uncertain about which model to trust, **combine** (equal weights as default). When you have strong theoretical or empirical reasons to prefer one model, use it alone --- but verify with the DM test.
:::

[*The M4 competition [@Makridakis2020]: top hybrid submissions combined diverse methods. Equal-weight ensembles ranked in the top 25% of all 61 participating methods.*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

# Key Takeaways and Roadmap

Five evaluation principles --- from metric choice to forecast combination --- that apply across the full course.

---

## Key Takeaways

::: {.keybox}
::: {.smaller}
1. **Metric choice is a business decision:** align RMSE/MAE/MASE with the cost structure; MAPE fails near zero.

2. **Walk-forward validation** gives a distribution of errors across origins --- one split is not sufficient.

3. **Diebold-Mariano test** determines whether accuracy differences are statistically real (use out-of-sample errors, HAC standard errors).

4. **Nested model comparisons** require the Clark-West correction; standard DM is invalid for nested models.

5. **Equal-weight combination** typically matches or beats the best individual model --- the combination puzzle.
:::
:::

[*We now have five model families + rigorous evaluation. The next step: machine learning for forecasting.*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## What's Next: Machine Learning for Forecasting

Classical forecasting is now complete: benchmarks $\to$ regression $\to$ ETS $\to$ ARIMA $\to$ multivariate $\to$ **evaluation**.

::: {.keybox}
**Lecture 7: Machine Learning Introduction** --- bias-variance tradeoff, train/validation/test discipline, and how cross-validation extends walk-forward ideas to ML models.
:::

::: {.smaller}

| **Aspect** | **Classical forecasting** | **ML forecasting** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Model structure | Specified (ARIMA, VAR) | Learned from data |
| Assumptions | Linearity, normality | Fewer formal (stationarity helpful) |
| Overfitting risk | Lower (few parameters) | High (mitigated by CV) |
| Evaluation | Walk-forward; DM test | Same + CV grid search |

:::

[**Lab 6:** walk-forward on RSXFS, DM test (SARIMA vs. ETS), forecast combination.]{.neutral .smaller}

---

## References {.smaller}

::: {#refs}
:::
